name: CA Blitzkrieg Pop
classification_notes: Deliberately blends serif and sans-serif letterforms in a single face, drawing on punk and grunge vernacular; best classed as display given its headline-first intent and raw, expressive character.
summary: A serif-meets-sans display face by Thomas Schostok rooted in '90s punk zine and DIY gig poster aesthetics.
description: Designed by Thomas Schostok at Cape Arcona, it fuses serif and sans-serif forms to channel the raw energy of grunge-era underground print. Three weights — Light, Regular and Bold — each with matching italics, are complemented by a variable font for continuous weight adjustment. Alternate characters and OpenType features add further latitude, making it suited to headlines, logos and short runs of punchy display text.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Blitzkrieg Pop as a rebellious mashup of serif and sans-serif channelling the raw, unfiltered energy of punk rock with a dash of Raygun-era cool. Born from the '90s underground of grunge zines and DIY gig posters, it is built for headlines, logos and short punchy bursts of text, offered in Light, Regular and Bold with matching italics and a variable font, plus alternate characters for extra riff and swagger.

name: CA Aires Pro
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Display Sans Serif; the design originated from all-caps letterforms on a 1930s postcard, giving it a distinctive period character with quirky, expressive forms suited to display use rather than text setting.
summary: A display sans-serif rooted in 1930s postcard lettering, expanded from its original all-caps format into a full character set with italic.
description: Originally drawn from the words 'BUENOS AIRES' spotted on a 1930s postcard stamp, the design began as an uppercase-only free font before growing into a full Pro release with lowercase letters and an italic. The expansion into lowercase was driven by requests from Spanish-speaking users, and the additional italic style adds expressive movement without abandoning the clean, idiosyncratic character of the originals. Two styles are supported across Western and Central European encodings, with a modest set of OpenType features including stylistic sets and fractions.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Aires Pro as having been inspired by the handwritten words 'BUENOS AIRES' found near the stamp on a 1930s postcard. The clean lines and quirky charm of the original uppercase design proved popular, prompting a full Pro version with lowercase letters that preserved the distinctive personality of the uppercase forms, later joined by an italic for added creative flexibility.

name: CA Aircona
classification_notes: Classified as Display by the foundry; styled after aircraft lettering with built-in 3D shadow and fill variants, placing it firmly in the decorative/display category.
summary: A three-style display face by Stefan Claudius modelled on aircraft lettering, with a layered Regular, Shadow and Fill system for dimensional typesetting.
description: Designed around the visual language of aviation lettering, CA Aircona comes in three complementary styles: Regular, Shadow and Fill. Overlaying the Regular with the Shadow style produces a convincing 3D effect, while the Fill variant adds further compositional flexibility. Upper and lowercase alternates are built in for additional variety when setting type.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Aircona as a font inspired by the lettering found on aircraft, created as an in-house exercise in designing an airline identity. The family includes upper and lowercase variations for typographic flexibility, and a 3D shadow system that works by duplicating and layering the Regular and Shadow styles.

name: CA Cape Rock
classification_notes: Classified as Display by the foundry; draws directly from the fat Clarendon tradition with heavy bracketed serifs and bold silhouettes intended for headline use.
summary: A fat Clarendon display face by Thomas Schostok and Stefan Claudius, built for heavy headlines with gritty, muscular presence and a generous set of OpenType alternates.
description: Rooted in the tradition of nineteenth-century fat Clarendons, CA Cape Rock brings bold, bracketed serifs and a heavy silhouette to display work. Two styles, Regular and Italic, are supported by discretionary ligatures, contextual alternates, swashes and stylistic sets, giving designers room to vary the texture of a headline or logotype. Coverage spans Western and Central European encodings, with 430 glyphs per style.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Cape Rock as a high-impact display font inspired by the heavy, timeless structures of fat Clarendons. They highlight its creative flexibility through numerous ligatures, unique terminal characters and fully functional OpenType features, positioning it as equally suited to spicing up a headline or anchoring a brand identity with vintage muscle and contemporary flair.

name: CA BND
classification_notes: Classified as sans-serif by the foundry; described as a DIN-like typeface, placing it firmly in the geometric/industrial sans tradition associated with German standards typography.
summary: A DIN-influenced geometric sans designed for the corporate identity of the German Intelligence Agency, offered in five styles including an alternate letterset and a distressed variant.
description: Designed by Thomas Schostok for the corporate image of a German intelligence agency, CA BND draws on the austere tradition of DIN typefaces, with an unemotional but quietly detailed character the foundry describes as a dangerous functionalism. The family runs to five styles: Regular, Bold, Alternate Regular, Alternate Bold and a Trash cut, the last introducing a distressed texture to the otherwise controlled forms. Western Latin encoding covers standard European language needs.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA BND as the product of their long-standing obsession with creating a DIN-like typeface, named after the German Intelligence Agency for which Thomas Schostok originally drew it as a corporate identity face. They characterise it as possessing a dangerous functionalism, unemotional on the surface yet harbouring details that reward closer attention, and it is offered in two styles alongside an alternate letterset and a distressed Trash variant.

name: CA Capoli
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Script; it is a display script with a hand-lettered, vintage Italian quality, drawn from a 1950s ceramic logo inscription rather than formal calligraphic tradition.
summary: A two-style vintage script drawn from a 1950s Italian ceramic ashtray logo, intended for illustrative titles and logotypes.
description: Designed by Thomas Schostok after discovering the word 'Nido' inscribed on a ceramic ashtray in an Italian bar, CA Capoli reconstructs what a full typeface from that fragment might have looked like. It comes in Regular and Stroke variants, covering Western and Central European character sets, with stylistic alternates and sets offering additional letter choices. The family has a warm, mid-century Italian character that suits branding, packaging and display lettering.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Capoli as a fine script typeface with a vintage touch, perfect for illustrative titles or logotypes. Its origin lies in a trip to Italy, where a ceramic ashtray bearing the word 'Nido' in what appeared to be a 1950s typeface inspired Thomas Schostok to reconstruct a full character set from just four letters. The foundry extended the project into a fictional hotel brand identity called Hotel Capoli, complete with business cards, key fobs and embroidered patches.

name: CA Coronado
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as Display Distressed; the rough, worn letterforms and dual-case alternates place it firmly in the display category with a distressed western aesthetic.
summary: A two-style distressed display face by Stefan Claudius with a worn, western character suited to headlines and logotypes.
description: Built around a rough, hand-worn aesthetic, CA Coronado comes in Regular and Shadow styles intended to work in combination, with the Shadow providing depth and the Regular serving as a fill layer. Upper and lower case letters share a visual kinship but differ in detail, with the uppercase running slightly more expressive and the lowercase more restrained, giving designers a set of stylistic alternates within a single weight. Coverage spans Western, Central European and Latin Extended encodings, with OpenType stylistic alternates included.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Coronado as a display typeface suited to headlines, logotypes and brands, giving it a used and authentic look through its rough style. The two styles, Regular and Shadow, are designed to be layered, with the Shadow adding extra impact and the Regular acting as a fill. Upper and lower cases appear similar at first glance but are distinct and can be used as alternates, with the uppercase being slightly more vivid.

name: CA Cula
classification_notes: Cape Arcona classify it as sans-serif; the design draws on industrial grotesque conventions similar to DIN while introducing rounder, humanist shapes and denaturalized ink traps, placing it between geometric and humanist sans traditions.
summary: A DIN-inflected sans by Thomas Schostok that replaces industrial rigidity with rounder forms and visible ink traps functioning as both text aid and display feature.
description: Rooted in the cool, utilitarian DNA of industrial faces like DIN, CA Cula introduces rounder, friendlier letterforms that soften the genre's characteristic coldness. Its denaturalized ink traps serve a dual role: at display sizes they read as a deliberate design detail that gives headlines a distinct character; at smaller sizes they perform their original function of preventing ink bleed and maintaining legibility. Five weights from Light to Superfat, each with a matching italic, keep the family lean and practical without the ambiguity of incremental semi-weights.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Cula as carrying familiar cool-tempered industrial DNA akin to DIN, but with rounder, friendlier shapes that make reading feel like a conversation rather than a chore. They highlight the denaturalized ink traps as a key feature, acting as a distinctive design element at large sizes and a functional ink-bleed solution at small sizes. The weight range is kept deliberately concise at five balanced options to avoid the semi-bold versus demi-bold confusion of larger families.

name: CA Edwald
classification_notes: Cape Arcona classifies it as serif; the design is a bold display roman drawing on the rounded, heavy forms of Cooper Black and the decorative qualities of Benguiat, making it a display serif in character.
summary: A bold display serif by Thomas Schostok and Stefan Claudius, conceived as a hybrid of Cooper Black and Ed Benguiat's Benguiat, available in five weights with italics and a variable font.
description: Drawing explicitly from two heavyweight American display classics, CA Edwald combines the rounded warmth of Cooper Black with the ornamental character of Benguiat into a single 10-style family. Five weights run from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, and a variable font is included alongside a rich set of OpenType features including swashes, stylistic alternates and titling alternates. Suited to advertising headlines, packaging and logo work where a distinctive period-inflected weight is needed.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Edwald as a superbly crafted alphabet design combining the familiar and the unusual with the practical and the aesthetic. They position it as the illegitimate child of Cooper Black and Benguiat, a heavy and solid typeface with a crisp, decisive look born for plain everyday advertising work, with assorted logo letters and headline details that add distinction to any campaign.

name: CA Emeralda
classification_notes: Cape Arcona classify it as Display Script; it combines an italic style drawn from a 1950s industrial advertisement headline with a complementary modern script, placing it firmly in retro display territory.
summary: A two-style display script family combining a 1950s advertisement-derived italic with a companion modern script.
description: The italic cut originated from a single headline spotted in an early-1950s industrial advertisement magazine, with Stefan Claudius expanding those few letterforms into a full retro-inflected script. A second style pairs it with a more contemporary script approach, giving the family a small but complementary range. Coverage spans Western encoding at 215 glyphs per style, suited to packaging, editorial headers and retro-themed branding.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Emeralda as a display script in two styles. The italic was sparked by a headline from an early-1950s industrial advertisement magazine; starting from just a handful of letters, it grew into a full retro font before being paired with a more modern script companion.

name: CA El Amor
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as Display Distressed; a reversed oblique headline face with vivid, crisp outlines designed for comic, Halloween and spooky contexts.
summary: A reversed oblique display face in two layerable styles, built for comics, Halloween and spooky graphic work.
description: Designed by Thomas Schostok, CA El Amor offers Regular and Fill styles intended for layered use, where the Fill sits behind or offset from the Regular outline to produce depth and dimension. The reversed oblique gives it a lively, energetic slant that suits poster and headline work rather than body copy. Despite its display focus, the character set is unusually broad for the genre, covering Central European and Western Latin encodings across 333 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA El Amor as a reversed oblique headline font made for experimentation with its two styles, Regular and Fill. They suggest overlaying the two on separate layers with or without offset, and note the Fill style can be used for body text at generous spacing, though kerning is optimised for use alongside the Regular. They position it as a natural fit for comics, Halloween and anything with a spooky edge, and highlight its unusually large character set for a display font.

name: CA Elvis in Stereo
classification_notes: The foundry classifies this as Display, and it is explicitly described as suitable for headlines and larger sizes only, with a single decorative style inspired by a 1972 live album cover aesthetic.
summary: A single-weight display typeface drawn from the visual world of a 1972 live Elvis Presley album recorded at Madison Square Garden.
description: Designed by Raymond Brekelmans and inspired by the typographic character of the Elvis at Madison Square Garden era, this is a display face built for large-scale use. It ships in one style with 341 glyphs and covers Western and Central European encodings. Its decorative nature makes it unsuitable for body text but well-suited to poster headlines and music-adjacent editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Elvis in Stereo as a typeface based upon the legendary live Elvis album recorded at Madison Square Garden in 1972, designed for headline and larger sizes.

name: CA Fourty Open
classification_notes: An all-caps neon-sign-style display face with a double-outline construction, designed explicitly for headlines rather than text use. The foundry classifies it as Display.
summary: A single-weight, all-caps display face built around a double-outline structure that evokes neon signage, arriving after 40 design iterations.
description: Thomas Schostok developed this typeface through 40 attempts to nail a neon-sign aesthetic, landing on a double-outline construction that gives letterforms a strong, open quality suited to headlines. Although it is an all-caps design, the uppercase and lowercase characters differ in openness, adding subtle rhythm. It shares a base with CA BND and pairs well with the double-lined script CA Capoli, and supports Western and Central European character sets across 365 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Fourty Open as a font that earned its name honestly, having taken 40 attempts to achieve the neon-sign style they were after. The double outline creates a strong, distinctive look for headlines, and because it shares a base with CA BND, the two work well together. The all-caps character set includes subtle differences between uppercase and lowercase letterforms, with the uppercase being more open, and the family covers Western and Central European encodings.

name: CA Geheimagent
classification_notes: Cape Arcona classifies it as sans serif; its origin as a prohibition sign in an industrial harbour setting, combined with strict upright uppercase forms and a distinctly angled italic, places it in an industrial sans subcategory.
summary: A four-style sans derived from industrial harbour prohibition signage, balancing utilitarian strictness with a dynamic italic.
description: Sitting between text and headline use, CA Geheimagent draws its character from prohibition signs in an industrial harbour context. The upright styles carry a rigid, institutional quality, while the italic cuts forward at an angle and introduces subtle differences beyond mere slant. Four styles cover Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic, with OpenType features including discretionary ligatures, stylistic alternates and tabular figures.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Geheimagent as neither a pure text font nor a pure headline font, positioning it as something in between. The italic style is presented as a counterpoint to the strictness of the roman, offering speed and forward motion. The typeface traces its origin to a prohibition sign in an industrial harbour, a heritage the foundry says gives it resilience and character.

name: CA Gothique
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as Display Sans Serif; positioned aesthetically between American Gothics and European Grotesques, with a single ultra-heavy weight intended for headline use.
summary: A single ultra-heavy display grotesque sitting between American Gothic and European Grotesque traditions, with small caps, four number formats and Central European character support.
description: CA Gothique is a Superfat-weight display sans by Stefan Claudius, designed to occupy a midpoint between American wood-type gothics and European grotesques. Despite offering only one weight, it punches well above its station through typographic utility: small caps, small caps numerals, four number formats and a Central European character set across 612 glyphs make it usable beyond pure headline work. The foundry classifies it as Display Sans Serif, and its single style keeps the proposition simple and deliberate.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Gothique as aesthetically positioned between American Gothics and European Grotesques, noting that its small caps, Central European character set and four number formats plus small caps numerals make it not only a headline font but an extremely versatile one. It is offered as a single Superfat style under a perpetual licence.

name: CA Kometo
classification_notes: Classified as Display by the foundry; a two-style headline face built around a shadow-and-fill stacking concept, designed for expressive, decorative use rather than text setting.
summary: A two-style layered headline face designed for stacking a shadow style over a fill style to create a retro, comic-book-inflected effect.
description: Built around two complementary styles, Regular (the shadow) and Fill, CA Kometo is intended to be used in layers, with the Fill placed beneath the Regular at a slight offset to generate a drop-shadow effect. The face carries an overtly playful, superhero-referencing personality suited to posters, packaging and bold editorial moments. Spacing and kerning are optimised for the stacked configuration rather than standalone text use. Coverage extends to Western and Central European Latin encodings across 344 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Kometo as a superhero typeface with the powers of shadow and imperfection, designed to rescue designers from boredom. It comes in two styles, Regular (shadow) and Fill, intended to be stacked on separate layers for a layered drop-shadow effect, with support for Western and Central European languages.

name: CA Kink
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly classifies CA Kink as Display Techno, and its origins as a book cover headline font with space-theme suitability confirm a narrow display application with a mechanistic or futuristic character.
summary: A single-weight techno display face by Thomas Schostok, originally drawn for a book cover and suited to space-themed editorial and headline work.
description: Designed initially for a book cover, CA Kink occupies a narrow but clear niche as a headline typeface with a techno or sci-fi inflection. A single Regular style covers Western and Central European encodings across 207 glyphs. Its tight scope makes it a focused choice for poster, title or branding contexts where a space-adjacent aesthetic is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Kink as originally created for a book cover, classifying it as Display Techno and noting its particular suitability as a headline font and for space-themed projects. It is designed by Thomas Schostok and supports Western and Central European encodings.

name: CA Hail to the King
classification_notes: Classified as Display by the foundry; the typeface is drawn from handmade signage letterforms worldwide, with deliberate irregularities in size and weight between cases that place it firmly outside conventional text categories.
summary: A single-weight display face assembled from handmade signs found across the world, with deliberately mismatched weights between upper and lowercase letters.
description: Drawn from hand-lettered signage sourced globally, the typeface deliberately mixes bold uppercase characters with regular-weight lowercase ones, creating an unpredictable, vernacular rhythm across any setting. It was originally created for an exhibition catalogue tied to the show 'Hail to the King, Baby!', and carries that one-off, found-lettering energy into a usable digital form. The character set spans Western and Central European encodings, with 367 glyphs including stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Hail to the King as a typeface based on handmade signs from around the world, created exclusively for an exhibition catalogue. Irregular character sizes and mixed weight styles between upper and lowercase make it varied and unpredictable, and the set includes Western and Central European diacritics alongside stylistic alternates.

name: CA Mechano
classification_notes: An all-caps mechanical typeface classified by the foundry as Display; its rigid geometric skeleton, width variants and disorder stylistic set place it firmly in the constructed display category rather than any text serif or sans tradition.
summary: An all-caps mechanical display typeface in three widths, with a disorder stylistic set that introduces controlled chaos into the rigid skeleton.
description: Built on a single mechanical skeleton, the family spans Regular, Condensed and Wide cuts, giving it range across headlines, signage and branding without breaking visual consistency. Two stylistic sets extend its character: one introduces a disruptive disorder mode that scrambles the expected geometry, the other softens things with rounded alternates. Western and Central European encodings are supported, and the glyph set runs to 396 characters.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Mechano as a mechanical, all-caps typeface that is straightforward to use by default, but activating the disorder stylistic set rapidly produces unsettling, expressive results; a second stylistic set offers rounded alternates as a calmer counterpoint. The three styles, Regular, Condensed and Wide, share the same mechanical skeleton and are suited to headlines, signage and branding.

name: CA Magic Hour
classification_notes: Uppercase-only vintage display face with a modernist, supersonic-era aesthetic. Cape Arcona classify it as Display, and the monospaced-width letterforms across all weights reinforce its geometric, retro-futurist character.
summary: An uppercase-only display face drawn from 1970s Concorde-era optimism, notable for keeping every letter at an identical width across all four weights.
description: Originally released in 2004 and revisited in 2026, CA Magic Hour adds Thin and Black to its original Bold, giving the family four weights from hairline to heavy. The defining feature is fixed character width: switching between Thin, Regular, Bold and Black leaves the text rhythm completely unchanged, an unusual constraint that suits typesetting where layout consistency matters. The face covers Western and Central European encodings and ships as a variable font alongside the four static styles.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Magic Hour as a vintage, uppercase-only typeface born in 2004 and revisited in 2026, drawing on the supersonic optimism of the Concorde era. The 2026 revision expands the original Bold into a four-weight family — Thin, Regular, Bold and Black — with each weight sharing identical letter widths, so the rhythm of a text block remains untouched regardless of which style is applied. It is available as both static fonts and a variable font.

name: CA Moskow Has A Plan
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as a distressed serif. The typeface mimics the imperfect, uneven impression of letterpress printing, with deliberately varied stroke weights and crippled outlines that evoke aged type.
summary: A single-weight distressed serif by Stefan Claudius, designed to replicate the uneven impression of letterpress printing with Cold War-era Russian character.
description: Inspired by an old Russian book about Moscow's plans for world domination, the typeface mimics hand-lettered letterpress work through deliberately varied stroke weights and roughened outlines. At small sizes it reads cleanly as a text font; at display sizes the imperfections become visible and expressive. The character set covers Western Latin, Central European Latin and Cyrillic, and the foundry recommends disabling kerning for a more authentic Cold War print feel.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Moskow Has A Plan as a font inspired by an old Russian book about Moscow's plan to take over the world, designed to give digital prints the feel of hand lettering. Its vivid outlines and slight variation in boldness between characters create a realistic, imperfect letterpress look. They note it works well as a text font at small sizes, with its distressed character becoming more apparent at larger sizes, and suggest turning off kerning for a full Cold War letterpress simulation.

name: CA Mystery Girl
classification_notes: An all-caps distressed typeface classified by the foundry as Display Distressed, designed to evoke the textural irregularities of silkscreen and letterpress printing through randomised OpenType alternates.
summary: An all-caps distressed display typeface by Thomas Schostok that simulates the imperfections of silkscreen and letterpress printing through pseudo-random OpenType alternates.
description: Built on a sturdy all-caps structure, the family uses a randomised stylistic alternates feature to introduce the kind of subtle variation that letterpress and silkscreen printing produce by chance. Two styles, Regular and Italic, cover Western and Central European Latin encodings, making it usable across a wide range of European languages. At its best in headline and poster work where a handcrafted, printed quality is wanted without sacrificing legibility.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Mystery Girl as a sturdy distressed all-caps typeface that captures the happy accidents of silkscreen and letterpress printing. A pseudo-random OpenType feature cycles through alternative letterforms to keep the texture feeling genuinely uneven, and extended Latin character support means it works across Western and Central European languages including Icelandic, Turkish, French, German and Polish.

name: CA Negroni
classification_notes: Classified by Cape Arcona as Display; a heavy hand-lettering revival rooted in early 20th-century advertising typography, with bold, unconventional letterforms that place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A heavy display typeface inspired by the hand-drawn advertising lettering of Wilhelm Deffke, bridging early 20th-century graphic vernacular with contemporary use.
description: Drawn in tribute to Wilhelm Deffke, a pioneer of German graphic design whose hand-lettered advertising work produced striking and unconventional forms, this typeface channels that atmosphere while stripping out distracting irregularities. Five styles span Light, Black, Round, Inline and Fill, with two stylistic sets offering alternative letterforms that shift the tone between playful and formal. Central European language support extends its reach beyond Western Latin.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Negroni as an allrounder between Neo and Retro, rooted in early 20th-century advertising typography where letterforms were often drawn by hand. The design pays tribute to Wilhelm Deffke and his characteristic heavy letterings, preserving unconventional charm while bringing the style to a contemporary level. Three additional styles and stylistic alternates give designers flexibility, and extensive Central European language support is included.

name: CA No Dr.
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as sans serif; drawn from a 1962 James Bond poster lettering, the design has a squared, constructed character with DIN-adjacent geometry and two widths that reinforce its geometric sans credentials.
summary: A geometric sans family drawn from the lettering on the 1962 James Bond: Dr. No poster, extended into a ten-style family with two widths and two weights.
description: Designed by Thomas Schostok, the family takes its cue from the original hand-lettered Dr. No title treatment and develops it into a functional range of ten styles. Two widths, standard and expanded, each carry Regular and Medium weights with matching italics, plus two additional cuts named Alarm and Tall for more expressive use. The foundry intended it as an alternative to DIN Schrift, though it has grown into a distinct family suited to headlines and capable of body text.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe No Dr. as inspired by the lettering for the 1962 movie poster James Bond: Dr. No, noting it shares the original's diabolical charm. The family was developed to combine distinctiveness with versatility across two widths and two weights, initially conceived as an alternative to the widely used DIN Schrift before evolving into a functional family in its own right. It is recommended primarily for headlines but works for body text too.

name: CA Normal
classification_notes: CA Normal draws explicitly from European grotesques and American gothic traditions, blending both into a neutral, clean sans-serif. The oblique follows grotesque convention rather than a separately drawn italic, reinforcing its grotesque heritage.
summary: A clean grotesque by Stefan Claudius that blends European and American gothic influences into a near-neutral text face with a few quietly distinctive details.
description: Designed to read without drawing attention to itself, CA Normal keeps its concept firmly in the background while introducing subtle, slightly uneven details that maintain optical rhythm in text. The oblique is slanted from the roman in true grotesque fashion, and a reverse oblique adds a cartographic oddity rarely seen outside map typography. Underneath, the family is well-furnished with small caps, proportional lining figures switchable to old-style or tabular via OpenType, and a Central European character set across 15 styles including a 'Left' sub-family of five weights.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Normal as aiming for beauty without tricks, pursuing clarity and well-balanced proportions by pulling from both European grotesque and American gothic traditions. They note that true neutrality may be unachievable, but consider CA Normal closer to it than most, with small intentional irregularities beneath the clean surface to keep the reader's eye engaged.

name: CA Oskar
classification_notes: Cape Arcona classify it as sans serif; its very slim compressed and condensed proportions with industrial character place it firmly in the grotesque tradition, comparable to Alternate Gothic.
summary: A compressed and condensed grotesque originally designed as a custom typeface for the Traumzeit music festival, balancing industrial narrowness with rounded forms.
description: Originating as a bespoke identity typeface for an international music festival, CA Oskar grew from compressed display caps into a full two-width, two-weight family with matching italics. Its very slim proportions across Compressed and Condensed cuts make it highly space-efficient, while a mix of angular rigour and rounded details gives it more personality than comparable narrow grotesques such as Alternate Gothic. Both families support Western and Central European encodings and include OpenType features for fractions and lining figures.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Oskar as a custom typeface created for the Traumzeit music festival's new corporate identity, designed to be characteristic yet flexible. They frame it as a mixture of industrial harshness and friendly round forms that reflect the festival's spirit of fusion, and position its slim two-width structure as a more characterful alternative to fonts like Alternate Gothic.

name: CA Normal Serif
classification_notes: Positioned by the foundry between modern and slab-serif, with influences from Chauncey H. Griffith's legibility group and scotch romans, placing it in a transitional zone with slab-adjacent detailing and condensed proportions.
summary: A serif companion to CA Normal that sits between modern and slab-serif traditions, drawing on Griffith's legibility group and scotch roman proportions.
description: Designed by Stefan Claudius as a conceptual match to the grotesque CA Normal rather than a straightforward serif counterpart, it shares proportions and quirkiness without being an obvious relative. The design picks up the abrasive details and slabbish serifs of classic newspaper type in the vein of Chauncey H. Griffith's legibility group, but with more condensed proportions that lend it a scotch-roman elegance. Ten styles run from Regular to Extra Bold, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Normal Serif as a companion to CA Normal that preserves principal proportions and a sense of quirkiness without aiming to be immediately identifiable as a relative. Situated between modern and slab-serif, it picks up elements of classic newspaper type from Griffith's legibility group, sharing abrasive details and slabbish serifs, while its more condensed proportions bring it closer to the aesthetic of scotch romans.

name: CA Postal
classification_notes: Classified as distressed by the foundry; the irregular, stamp-like outlines and contextual alternates place it firmly in the display category, evoking moveable rubber stamp printing sets rather than any conventional text type genre.
summary: A single-weight stamp-effect display face with contextual alternates that prevent identical characters appearing adjacent, maintaining an authentic rubber-stamp irregularity.
description: Designed by Stefan Claudius and originally created for a record cover, CA Postal draws its inspiration from moveable stamp printing sets with a Futura-adjacent geometric quality. The irregular outlines are its defining trait, and two sets of characters combined with the Contextual Alternates OpenType feature ensure no two identical glyphs sit side by side. At 450 glyphs, it covers Western and Central European encodings with discretionary ligatures and stylistic alternates alongside the core alternates logic.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Postal as a cute and clever stamp-font, initially made for a record cover before being expanded into a full typeface when lyrics needed setting in a booklet. The inspiration was a moveable stamp printing set with a pleasant Futura-like quality, readable even at small sizes, but it is the loose and irregular outlines that give the font its charm. The Contextual Alternates feature ensures no two identical characters appear next to each other, making the stamp effect feel more authentic.

name: CA Rebel Party Rebel
classification_notes: Classified as Display by the foundry; a handmade, expressive two-style typeface with a deliberately rough, rebellious character that places it firmly outside text-setting categories.
summary: A two-style handmade display typeface born from a story of rage and a thrown television, offering a thin and a fat cut for deliberately irreverent typography.
description: Created by Thomas Schostok under reportedly furious circumstances, the family comes in just two styles: CA PartyRebel, a thin cut, and CA RebelParty, a heavier counterpart. The handmade quality gives both styles an unpolished, agitated texture suited to editorial moments, poster work or anywhere a studied crudeness is the point. Western Latin coverage with 240 glyphs per style, sold as a perpetual licence for a one-time fee.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe the typeface as born from hate and anger after Thomas Schostok threw a television from the 14th floor of a hotel near Austin, Texas. CA PartyRebel is the thin style for telling everyone you are a rebel at any party; CA RebelParty is the fat style, insisting that every party must be rebellious. Together they are offered as a reminder of the indifference of the mainstream.

name: CA Play
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Display Script; the family spans semi-connected casual scripts and italic variants with a handwritten, retro-inflected character across its five styles.
summary: A five-style casual script family with handwritten warmth, ranging from clipped roman cuts to fully connected flowing forms with a mid-century European poster feel.
description: The family splits across five distinct styles: Real carries long flowing tails for a looser handwritten quality, Roman trims them for text legibility, Script connects letters into a continuous flow, Wild pushes expressive looseness further, and Dynamic offers a straightforward italic slant. Wild and Script in particular carry a strong retro charge, recalling European film posters and title sequences from the 1950s and 60s. With 208 glyphs and Western encoding, it suits film and TV work, poster design and on-screen text that needs period-appropriate personality.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Play as a typeface that invites experimentation across five distinct personalities. Real offers flowing tails and a handwritten looseness, Roman tightens that into something more readable, Script connects letters continuously, Wild goes furthest with expressive forms, and Dynamic is a clean italic for when you need slant without transformation. Wild and Script are noted for their retro European film poster energy, making them particularly suited to vintage-styled title sequences and poster work.

name: CA Rough Rider
classification_notes: A bold italic display typeface with a deliberately rough, worn texture; Cape Arcona classify it as Distressed, and its single-weight italic construction is built entirely for headline and logotype use.
summary: A single-weight bold italic display face with a rough, worn texture, originating from a one-off logo commission and expanded to cover Western and Central European character sets.
description: Designed by Stefan Claudius, CA Rough Rider began life as a bespoke logotype before being extended into a full typeface. The rough, hand-drawn quality carries through at display sizes without becoming illegible at smaller settings. With 341 glyphs and OpenType stylistic alternates, it supports Western and Central European encodings and suits title work, branding and editorial headlines where grit is the point.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Rough Rider as a handcrafted bold italic with a rough, worn-in edge, built as a display style from the outset. It began as a one-off logo job but had enough character to warrant expansion into a full typeface with Central European support, making it usable across full projects rather than a single commission.

name: CA Recape
classification_notes: Classified as Script by the foundry; drawn from American 1950s lettering traditions with a distressed variant, placing it firmly in the decorative vintage script category.
summary: A two-style vintage script family by Thomas Schostok, drawn from a selective remix of American 1950s lettering forms rather than any single historical source.
description: Rather than reviving one period typeface, Thomas Schostok pulled the most distinctive letter forms from 1950s American lettering and merged them into a single family. The Regular style carries a polished, hand-lettered quality, while the Raw variant simulates the look of poorly printed type for added texture and age. OpenType features include underlining swashes, stylistic and contextual alternates, discretionary ligatures and ornaments, making it well suited to logotypes, headlines and poster work.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Recape as a weird and beautiful vintage script family, inspired by American 1950s lettering but functioning as a best-of remix rather than a straight revival. It merges the oddest and most beautiful letter forms of the era into one charming bastard of a font, packed with OpenType features and accompanied by a Raw style that replicates the distressed look of a poorly printed typeface.

name: CA Saygon Display
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as sans serif, but its origins in expressing postmodern rupture and its deliberately idiosyncratic letterforms place it firmly in the display category rather than functional sans territory.
summary: A display sans conceived from a corporate project that never launched, shaped by a deliberate tension between individual rebellion and typographic uniformity.
description: Designed by Thomas Schostok and Stefan Claudius, CA Saygon Display grew out of hundreds of discarded sketches during an unrealised corporate identity project. The three-weight family uses unconventional letterforms intended to make visible the fractures of a postmodern society, while still cohering as a usable typeface. Spacing and kerning were handled by Igino Marini using iKern, and the glyph set covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Vietnamese with a full complement of OpenType features including fractions, superiors and stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Saygon Display as originating in a large corporate design project that ultimately never came to fruition. The goal was a typeface that would make the ruptures of postmodern society visible, with a handful of letters chosen after hundreds of discarded sketches. The combination of those letters, they say, makes the rebellion of individuality visible while still yielding a uniform typeface. Language support covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Vietnamese, with kerning carried out by Igino Marini via iKern.

name: CA Segundo
classification_notes: Cape Arcona classify it as Display; the typeface's defining trait is its alternating stroke contrast across characters, mixing fat and thin strokes in round letters and two orthogonal styles in others, making it too idiosyncratic for standard grotesque classification.
summary: A single-weight display face inspired by Cuban wall lettering, built around a deliberate mixing of fat and thin strokes within the same alphabet.
description: Stefan Claudius took the visual inconsistency of a Cuban wall-writing and turned it into a governing system: round characters such as O, U and C carry either a fat or thin stroke, while characters with orthogonal lines switch between two distinct styles. Uppercase letters stress the vertical; lowercase stress the horizontal. The result is a typeface where stylistic variation is baked into the glyph set rather than applied after the fact, supported by stylistic sets and alternates via OpenType.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Segundo as born from a piece of Cuban wall-writing that initially appeared to contain errors. On closer inspection, it was simply mixing stroke styles, and that discovery became the core design principle: round letters offer a choice between fat and thin strokes, while straight-sided characters come in two orthogonal variants, so that the act of typing becomes an act of design.

name: CA Slalom
classification_notes: Cape Arcona classifies it as sans serif; its rounded forms, deep stroke-end cuts and references to Gill Sans and Antique Olive place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition with a contemporary inflection.
summary: A 42-style humanist sans by Stefan Claudius spanning four widths, drawn from the spirit of Gill Sans and Antique Olive with a distinctively rounded, restrained character.
description: Designed by Stefan Claudius, CA Slalom spans four widths — Compressed, Condensed, Normal and Extended — each sharing the same underlying design logic across six weights with matching italics, plus a variable font. Its defining traits are rounded forms, deeply tucked stroke-ends and a slightly lower x-height than expected, details that accumulate into a recognisable personality without shouting. The family covers Latin Extended and Cyrillic encodings and ships with a thorough OpenType feature set including multiple figure styles and stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Slalom as a contemporary take on the spirit of classics like Gill and Antique Olive, rebuilt for how type is used today. The S became the breakout shape, its curve so distinctive it anchored the whole family. Working outward from an extra bold extended starting point, the family grew to four widths with a shared underlying logic — round rather than square, with deep stroke-ends and a slightly lower x-height — restrained enough, they suggest, to age as well as the classics that inspired it.

name: CA Saygon Text
classification_notes: Inspired by early static grotesques such as Akzidenz Grotesk, CA Saygon Text is a text-oriented grotesque sans with a high x-height, right-angled terminals, and multiple stylistic alternates referencing Helvetica, Futura, Franklin Gothic and bespoke forms.
summary: A text-optimised grotesque with seven weights and five stylistic alternate sets that let users switch between single- and double-storey letterforms inspired by Helvetica, Futura and Franklin Gothic.
description: Developed as a less eccentric companion to CA Saygon, this family retains the parent's proportions while adopting a higher x-height, reduced ascenders, a low cap height and right-angled terminals on f and t. Five named stylistic sets — Helvetica, Futura, Franklin, Cape and Flat — offer distinct combinations of a, g and y forms, making the typeface unusually adaptable for a single grotesque family. Latin Extended and Cyrillic coverage reaches as far as Vietnamese, and spacing was finished by Igino Marini using iKern.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Saygon Text as a sibling typeface to CA Saygon, developed to provide a more restrained option for extended text setting. Early static grotesques like Akzidenz Grotesk served as a fundamental inspiration, though the designers deliberately avoided a historical appearance, favouring contemporary details such as flat, right-angled terminals and a high x-height. Five stylistic sets — named Helvetica, Futura, Franklin, Cape and Flat — allow typographers to mix single- and double-storey a and g forms, with the Cape style's single-storey a paired with a three-storey g described as a genuine rarity.

name: CA Smut
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as Display Distressed; drawn from bold, gritty comic book advertising lettering of the 1950s and 1960s, designed as a layered shadow-and-fill system for horror and vintage poster work.
summary: A two-layer display typeface by Thomas Schostok, drawing on bold, gritty 1950s and 1960s comic book advertising lettering for horror poster and retro graphic work.
description: Built around a shadow-and-fill system, the family comprises two styles intended to be stacked: Regular provides the drop shadow layer and Fill provides the colour fill, allowing colour, offset and tracking to be played with independently. The design pulls from the rough, hand-lettered advertising styles found in mid-century American comic books, giving it an appropriately sinister texture suited to horror film posters and vintage editorial work. Spacing and kerning are optimised for use with both styles together, though the Fill layer can be used in isolation for more unpredictable results.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Smut as a font whose dirtiness is its charm, inspired by the bold, gritty advertising lettering of 1950s and 1960s comic books. The two styles, Regular (the shadow) and Fill (the filling), are designed to be layered and played with using colour, offset and tracking to create small masterpieces. They suggest it is indispensable for anyone looking to design a horror movie poster.

name: CA Spotnik
classification_notes: Classified as sans serif by the foundry, with slight stroke contrast and wide proportions; the hand-drawn inspiration and quirky details place it firmly in the grotesque tradition.
summary: A wide grotesque with slight contrast, drawn from a hand-lettered title card in an early Tarkovsky film, with a distinctive swirly alternate 's'.
description: Inspired by a hand-drawn title sequence from an early Andrei Tarkovsky film, CA Spotnik is a grotesque sans with subtle stroke contrast and wide, generous proportions built around a large x-height. The family spans six weights from Thin to Extrabold, each with a matching italic, and grows progressively wider as the weight increases. A variable font is included alongside the static styles, and a standard 's' is available via stylistic sets for those who prefer to skip the signature swirling alternate.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Spotnik as a grotesque typeface born from a screenshot of an unconventional hand-drawn 's' in an early Tarkovsky opening title. They characterise its atmosphere as 'nerdy but friendly', a quality they attribute not to any single detail but to the accumulated effect of all the decisions drawn from that original source. Twelve styles plus a variable font are available, with support for Western, Central European and Latin Extended encodings.

name: CA Spotnik Serif
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as serif; influences from Scotch Roman and Scotch Serif traditions are evident, particularly in the italic, though the absence of traditional vertical serifs gives it a distinctly contemporary character.
summary: A contemporary serif by Stefan Claudius that draws on Scotch Roman traditions while deliberately avoiding conventional vertical serifs for a cleaner, narrower form.
description: Ten styles span Thin to Extrabold with matching italics, plus a variable font, making it adaptable for both body text and larger display use. The narrower proportions give it an economy well suited to extended reading, and the elegant italic retains the classic Scotch Serif flavour the designer cites as an influence. Extensive OpenType support includes stylistic alternates, fractions, old-style figures, superiors and inferiors, and multiple numeral sets.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Spotnik Serif as a distinctive serif with no direct historical predecessors, notable for its absence of traditional vertical serifs. They cite influence from Scotch Serif fonts, particularly visible in the italic, and position it as an evolution of the CA Spotnik grotesque family, refined with narrower proportions for legible body text use. Stylistic alternates and broad OpenType functionality are highlighted as tools for fine-tuning typographic flavour.

name: CA Spy Royal
classification_notes: A junctionless display script drawing on mid-20th century streamline lettering and commercial signage; the foundry classifies it as Display Script, sitting between classic script and retro streamline type.
summary: A junctionless display script by Thomas Schostok rooted in 1954 Japan Airlines lettering, expanded into six styles including shadow and line variants.
description: Originally derived from a single handful of uppercase letters spotted in a 1954 Japan Airlines advertisement, the family was extended into a full character set and first released in 2004 before undergoing a significant rework. Six styles cover Regular, two shadow treatments, Shadow Raw, Line and Line Bold, giving designers options for depth and dimension in vintage or retro contexts. It has found use on cocktail bottles, racing posters, bar menus and hot rod merchandise, reflecting its strong mid-century commercial vernacular.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe Spy Royal as a junctionless script sitting between a classic script and the streamline fonts of the mid-20th century, with its starting point being a 1954 Japan Airlines advertisement for flights aboard the DC-6B Pacific Courier. First released in 2004 and subsequently reworked, the family now spans six styles including shadow-line and 3D-shadow cuts, and is suited to anything with a retro edge — cars, racing, cocktails, vintage restaurants and airlines.

name: CA Superpilot
classification_notes: The sans component is described as a modern interpretation of Futura, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition. The foundry classifies the overall family as Script, Sans Serif, and Display.
summary: A 32-style typeface duo pairing a geometric Futura-derived sans with a monoline script drawn from vintage 1950s camera equipment lettering.
description: The sans component spans five weights from Light to Black with matching italics across normal, condensed and expanded widths, following the proportions and spirit of Futura. The script component references logos and type designations found on mid-century camera equipment, producing a monoline style suited to uppercase headline settings despite its calligraphic origins. Both subfamilies share Central European and Western character sets, contextual alternates and stylistic sets, and the full family is available as a variable font.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Superpilot as a must-have typeface duo for designers and creatives: a clean, contemporary sans rooted in Futura paired with a vintage-inspired monoline script based on 1950s camera equipment lettering. Together they offer versatility across branding, print and web projects, with extensive Central European character support, alternate characters and a variable font included across all 32 styles.

name: CA Telecopy
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as Techno Display; the typeface deliberately mimics pixelated, glitched output from early 1990s fax machines and low-resolution monitors, placing it firmly in the display category with a distressed digital aesthetic.
summary: A deliberately degraded display typeface that reproduces the pixelated distortion of early 1990s fax machines and CRT monitors across three quality levels and four weights.
description: Designed by Thomas Schostok, CA Telecopy comes in three subfamilies, A, B and C, each simulating a different pixel resolution at 40, 24 and 15 pixels respectively, giving twelve styles in total across Light, Regular, Medium and Bold. The glitch aesthetic is treated as an intentional design choice rather than a flaw, drawing on the visual language of early internet artefacts and analogue-to-digital degradation. With 554 glyphs per style and support for Latin Extended and Central European encodings, it suits editorial, branding and screen work where retro digital noise is the point.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Telecopy as a celebration of the imperfections of the early 90s, capturing the chunky pixels and distorted characters of fax machines and low-resolution monitors. They frame the typeface as a statement piece that revels in digital mistakes, with three quality levels simulating pixel heights of 40, 24 and 15, and four weights per level giving twelve styles in total.

name: CA Trasher
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as Display Distressed; all letterforms are constructed from dots, giving the typeface a deliberately rough, handmade quality suited to display use only.
summary: A single-weight display face by Thomas Schostok in which every letterform is built entirely from dots, with some uppercase characters offering alternative shapes.
description: Each glyph in CA Trasher is hand-constructed from dots, giving the family a rough, textural quality that sits firmly in the decorative display category. The 163-glyph set covers Western Latin encoding, with select uppercase letters providing alternate character shapes for added variety. Best suited to headlines, poster work or branding where a raw, handcrafted aesthetic is the point.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Trasher as a typeface where all letters are handmade out of dots, with some uppercase letters offering alternative character shapes. It is available as a single Regular style and classified under Display Distressed.

name: CA Uruguay
classification_notes: Classified as Display by the foundry; an uppercase-only headline face with ink traps as a defining stylistic feature rather than a functional weight distinction, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: An uppercase-only display face by Thomas Schostok, offered in two styles where the 'Light' variant substitutes ink traps for a reduced stroke weight.
description: Two styles make up CA Uruguay, both uppercase only and built for headline use. Rather than a conventional light-to-regular weight progression, the lighter style achieves its differentiation through an exaggerated double portion of ink traps, which at large sizes reads as an unusual and visually arresting texture. The foundry cites uses ranging from national football team branding to South American aircraft carrier lettering, suggesting a face suited to bold, institutional or sporting contexts.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Uruguay as an original headline font available exclusively in uppercase, with two styles that differ not through conventional weight variation but through ink trapping — the lighter cut carries a double portion of ink traps, making it unusual and beautiful at display sizes. They suggest applications as diverse as the Uruguayan national football team and South American aircraft carrier lettering.

name: CA Viva Las Vegas
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Display Techno; it is a layered light-bulb font directly referencing 1970s Las Vegas concert hall signage, built for large-scale decorative use rather than text setting.
summary: A layered light-bulb display font in two stackable styles, drawn from the neon and incandescent concert hall signage of 1970s Las Vegas.
description: Built on a Day and Night system across five weights from Ultra Light to Bold, the two styles are designed to be stacked and colour-mixed for a wide range of theatrical combinations. With 574 glyphs per style, Western, Central European and Cyrillic encoding, and a variable font option, it has reasonable range for a display face. It performs best at large sizes where the individual bulb details read clearly.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Viva Las Vegas as a light bulb font inspired by the concert hall signage of 1970s Las Vegas, the kind you would have seen when Elvis was playing the strip. It comes in two styles, Night and Day, across five weights, built on a layered system so users can stack styles and mix colours for a huge range of combinations. They recommend using it at large sizes.

name: CA Weird Stories
classification_notes: Classified by Cape Arcona as Display Distressed; an irregular, slanted display face designed for stacking two complementary styles to produce layered special effects, placing it firmly in expressive display territory.
summary: A two-style layered display face by Thomas Schostok designed to be stacked for a distressed, otherworldly special-effects result.
description: The Regular and Fill styles are built to overlay one another, producing a shadowed, dimensional effect when combined. Used separately, the Fill style reads as deliberately uneven, which is by design rather than accident. Western and Central European encodings are supported across 304 glyphs per style, making it workable for science fiction, horror and Halloween contexts.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Weird Stories as a font from outer space, suited to science fiction novels and spooky atmospheres. They highlight the ability to stack the Regular and Fill styles on top of each other to create special effects, noting that the Fill style alone may look uneven since it was designed for use in combination with the shadow/regular style.

name: CA Wolkenfluff
classification_notes: Derived from the pixelated title font of a 1991 Amiga game, the letterforms are chunky and bitmap-influenced, placing it firmly in the display category with a retro pixel subcategory.
summary: A chunky display typeface derived from the pixelated title lettering of the 1991 Amiga game James Pond II, offered in four distinct style variants.
description: The letterforms trace directly back to a beloved 8-bit game title, giving the family an unapologetically fat, blocky character. Four styles cover the range from clean Regular through glossy Gloss and cut-out Stencil to the distressed Trash variant, letting designers choose how polished or roughed-up the result should feel. At 188 glyphs per style, coverage is Western Latin only, keeping the family focused on display and headline use.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Wolkenfluff as a fat, juicy sausage of a font whose shapes come from the pixelated title font in the 1991 Amiga game James Pond II: Codename Robocod. It comes in four styles — Regular, Gloss, Stencil and Trash — mixing clean and distressed finishes to suit whatever level of grit the job calls for.

name: CA Yoshiro
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as Sans Serif Display Techno; the design draws directly from the Eurostile and Microgramma lineage, featuring squared geometric letterforms associated with sci-fi and military visual language.
summary: A squared geometric display family by Thomas Schostok, positioned as a contemporary alternative to Eurostile and Microgramma for sci-fi, action and military titles.
description: With ten styles split across Normal and Wide subfamilies, CA Yoshiro offers designers the range of letter widths that Eurostile users historically had to fake through distortion. A variable font is included, allowing continuous width and weight adjustment within a consistent design. The Normal styles are legible enough for body text use, while the Wide styles replicate the extended proportions of Eurostile Extended for genre-appropriate headlines and titles.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Yoshiro as a modern answer to the shortcomings of classic sci-fi typefaces like Eurostile and Microgramma, citing the common practice of distorting those fonts as a design problem worth solving. The family offers futuristic extended styles alongside normalised variants suitable for body text, and includes a variable font so designers can fine-tune width and weight for any application from opening titles to streaming covers.

name: CA Zentrum
classification_notes: Classified as sans-serif by the foundry; the combination of bold, distinct letterforms with rounder shapes and tight spacing places it firmly in the grotesque tradition, with a contemporary geometric inflection.
summary: A five-weight grotesque sans in three widths, designed by Thomas Schostok with rounder forms that ease reading at smaller sizes without sacrificing a bold, distinct character.
description: Thirty styles span five weights from Light to Black across Normal, Condensed and Wide widths, each with matching italics, plus a variable font. The rounder internal shapes soften what would otherwise be a fairly assertive grotesque, making it workable for body text and editorial layouts as well as headlines. Glyph coverage extends to over 100 Latin languages and Cyrillic, with a strong OpenType feature set including old-style figures, fractions and contextual alternates.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Zentrum as mixing the concise with the practical: bold and distinct but still modern and approachable, with rounder shapes that aid readability on closer inspection. They position it for corporate branding, websites, editorial work and advertising, noting its tight, consistent spacing and comprehensive OpenType features as making it a solid choice for both body text and display use.

name: CA Yoshiro Pixel
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Display Techno; the typeface is built entirely from pixel grids with three distinct rendering styles (clean pixel, blur, round dot-matrix), placing it firmly in the display techno category.
summary: A pixel-grid display family in three rendering styles — clean, blurred and dot-matrix — designed for retro-tech and sci-fi aesthetics.
description: Built from pixel grids, CA Yoshiro Pixel extends the spirit of Cape Arcona's CA Yoshiro into three distinct subfamilies: the crisp Pixel, the thermally smeared Pixel Blur, and the dot-matrix Pixel Round. Each subfamily comes in Normal and Wide widths with Regular and Bold weights, totalling 12 styles, and the glyph set of over 1,000 characters covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic, Greek and Vietnamese. Aimed at game graphics, glitch art and futuristic branding, the family includes stylistic sets, contextual alternates and discretionary ligatures for expressive typesetting.
foundry_blurb: Cape Arcona describe CA Yoshiro Pixel as bringing retro pixel aesthetics together with a sci-fi sensibility, rebuilding the spirit of CA Yoshiro from the smallest digital building blocks. The family comes in three flavours — clean grid-perfect Pixel, hazy thermally-printed Pixel Blur, and friendly dot-matrix Pixel Round — each in Normal and Wide widths plus Regular and Bold weights, with over 1,000 glyphs and broad multilingual support.
